program: Travel English -- Europe Without a Translator
hub_subtitle: Travel English for the airport, the check-in counter, the hotel and passport control — so that Europe needs no translator

lesson.menu_title: What Were You Doing When It Happened? -- The Story Is Yours
lesson.menu_desc: Past continuous: the long action and the thing that cut it in half. The day the alarm went off, the bus broke down and the plane took off without you -- and how to tell it at the dinner table -- 35 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 11 -- What Were You Doing When It Happened?
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Rosângela | Lesson 11 -- What Were You Doing When It Happened?
lesson.grammar_point: past continuous for interrupted actions (was/were + -ing + when)
phases: The Day It All Went Wrong

listenings.text: So you want to hear about the worst day of my trip? All right. My name is Frank, and this happened to me in Rome, two years ago, and my wife still tells this story at every dinner party we go to. Our flight was at eleven in the morning. Eleven. We had a whole morning, and I was not worried at all. At seven o'clock I was sleeping like a baby, and my alarm did not go off. It did not go off because I set it for seven in the evening. Seven P M. So at half past eight my wife was shaking my shoulder and shouting my name, and that is how the day started. We were running to the taxi at a quarter to nine, and we were laughing, because we still had two hours, and two hours is a lot. Then we got on the highway, and we stopped. A traffic jam. A big one. We were sitting in that taxi for fifty minutes, and we were not moving one meter. At that point I was still calm. Then, all of a sudden, the engine made a noise like a sick animal, and the taxi broke down. Right there, in the middle of the traffic jam. The driver was looking at the engine and I was looking at my watch, and it was twenty past ten. I could not believe it. We took our bags and we walked. We walked with two suitcases on the side of a highway in Rome, and a bus stopped for us, and I am still thankful to that driver. At the airport, I was lining up at the desk when I realized my passport was in the front pocket of the other suitcase. The one my wife was carrying. We were rushing to the gate when the woman there closed the door. She closed it, and she looked at me, and she said, I am sorry, sir. And through the window we were watching our plane take off. Without us. So, what did I learn? Two things. Read your alarm, morning or evening. And leave early. That is all. Now everybody has heard this story a hundred times, and my wife tells it better than me.

Hello. My name is Teresa, I am seventy-one years old, and I want to tell you about a train station in Italy, because I think you need to hear this one. I started traveling alone when I was sixty-six. Before that, never. My husband did the talking for forty years, and then my husband was gone, and I thought, well, that is the end of Italy for me. It was not. So. Two years ago I was standing in the station in Florence, and I was waiting for the train to Bologna, and I was very proud of myself, because I was doing all of it alone. And then a voice said something in Italian, and everybody around me stood up and started walking away. Everybody. And I was sitting there with my little suitcase, and I did not understand one word. My train was cancelled. I found that out three minutes later, from a screen. Now, this is the part I want you to hear. The old me, the one with the husband, would have cried. Really. But I did not cry. I walked to the information desk, and there was a long line, and I lined up, and I waited twenty minutes, and I said the only thing I had: my train is cancelled, what should I do? Six words. That is all I had. And the young man behind the desk, he answered me slowly, because he saw my face, and he put me on a train forty minutes later, and he wrote the platform number on a piece of paper for me. Fourteen. I still have that piece of paper at home, in a drawer. And when I was sitting on that train to Bologna, I was looking out of the window, and I was crying a little bit then, but for the other reason. Because I did it. Nobody did it for me. So here is what I want to say to you. You are going to have a bad day. The train is going to be cancelled, and the alarm is not going to go off, and something is going to break down. That is not the problem. The problem is thinking that you need perfect English to survive a bad day. You do not. I had six words, and they were enough. Go.

structure.title: One long action, one short event, one word that glues them
cards: the LONG action -- it was already happening
"I was waiting at the gate." You did not start it in that second and you did not finish it in that second. It was already going on, like a line on a timeline.
the SHORT event -- it cut the line in half
"...they announced the delay." One second. It arrives, it happens, it is over. This is the tense you have used since Lesson 2.
the glue -- it says which one is which
"I was waiting at the gate WHEN they announced the delay." After WHEN comes the short one. After WHILE comes the long one. Same story, two doors.

items.situation: Somebody at the dinner table asks: what happened on your last trip? Do not give a list. Start the story with the long action -- where you were and what you were doing.
tips: I was waiting at the gate in Lisbon when the screen changed.
We were having dinner at the airport when they called my name.

items.situation: Your alarm did not work. Say it -- and say what you were doing at that moment.
tips: I was sleeping when my alarm went off. It went off at seven in the evening, not seven in the morning.
All of a sudden it was half past eight, and I was still sleeping.

items.situation: The taxi stopped working on the way to the airport. Tell that part.
tips: We were sitting in a traffic jam when the taxi broke down.
The taxi broke down while we were going to the airport, and we were stuck for fifty minutes.

items.situation: You were at the desk, and then you understood that your passport was in the other bag. Tell it with the two tenses.
tips: I was lining up at the desk when I realized my passport was in the other bag.
While I was lining up, I realized I did not have my passport.

items.situation: You ran, and you arrived, and the door was closed. The plane left without you. Say it.
tips: We were rushing to the gate when the woman closed the door. We missed it.
We were watching our plane take off, and we were still inside the airport. I could not believe it.
At that point I was not relieved. I was exhausted, and I was still in Rome.

items.situation: Now the good ending: the airline put you on the next flight, and it left on time. How did you feel, and what were you doing when they told you?
tips: I was lining up at the desk when the agent said there was a seat at four o'clock. I was so relieved.
The next flight took off on time, and I slept for three hours.

items.situation: Your friend says a story is only the facts: the flight was late, we waited, we arrived. You disagree. Say why.
tips: I disagree. Nobody wants the list -- they want to know what you were doing when it happened.
In my opinion, a story needs the long part and the short part. The list is not a story.

items.situation: Somebody asks you why you always tell the same story from Paris. Answer, and start it.
tips: Once in a while something goes wrong, and that is the only part anybody remembers.
I was standing at passport control in London when the officer asked me a question, and I was not ready for it.
